docs.title: DISC Lab Wiki
docs.text:
Welcome to the starter knowledge base for the Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction (DISC) Lab.

This site is designed as the lab's central documentation system. It should support:

  • research reproducibility
  • onboarding of new members
  • standardization of workflows
  • transfer of knowledge across projects
  • clear internal policies and expectations

docs.title: How this wiki should be used
docs.text: This wiki should function as the lab's single source of operational truth for recurring procedures, technical workflows, templates, and internal expectations.

docs.title: Maintenance ownership
docs.text: The PI or designated lab manager should approve structural changes. Students and staff can contribute updates through pull requests.

docs.title: Administrative Policies
docs.text: This section documents recurring operating rules for meetings, reporting, code review, and ownership expectations.

docs.title: Core rules
docs.text:
• no major codebase changes without review
  • use pull requests for important shared repositories
  • keep pull requests focused and reviewable
  • explain non-obvious design choices
  • verify that paths, secrets, and temporary files are not committed

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Project data, code, and outputs created under lab projects should be treated as institutional research assets subject to project terms, collaborator agreements, and institutional policy.

docs.title: Practical implications
docs.text:
• members should not remove shared project assets without authorization
  • departures must include proper handover of code, data, and documentation
  • external sharing requires approval when ownership or sensitivity is unclear

docs.title: Typical meeting structure
docs.text:
• weekly or biweekly project meetings
  • monthly strategic review when needed
  • short written updates before major meetings

docs.title: Expectation
docs.text: Members should arrive prepared, with clear progress, blockers, and next steps.

docs.title: Minimum reporting content
docs.text:
• what was done
  • what is blocked
  • what comes next
  • what decisions are needed

docs.title: Recommended cadence
docs.text: Adapt cadence to project phase, but avoid long periods without documented progress.

docs.title: AI and CV Best Practices
docs.text: This section captures lab-wide standards for AI, machine learning, and computer vision workflows.

docs.title: General rules
docs.text:
• define classes before labeling begins
  • document ambiguous cases with examples
  • perform quality checks on a sample of annotations
  • revise the protocol when repeated confusion appears

docs.title: For damage segmentation
docs.text: Document clearly: - class boundaries - thin damage representation rules - occlusion handling - mixed or overlapping damage cases - minimum visible evidence required for labeling

docs.title: Evaluation Metrics
docs.text: Choose metrics that match the engineering objective.

docs.title: Common metrics
docs.text:
• mAP: useful for detection and segmentation benchmarks
  • IoU / mIoU: useful for overlap-based segmentation quality
  • precision / recall / F1: useful when false negatives or false positives matter differently
  • pixel accuracy: only supplementary for imbalanced segmentation tasks

docs.title: Engineering note
docs.text: For damage tasks, report not only aggregate metrics but also: - per-class metrics - class imbalance discussion - qualitative examples - failure modes relevant to field use

docs.title: Reproducibility Checklist
docs.text:
Use this checklist before sharing results internally or externally.

  • Dataset version is identified
  • Train/validation/test split is fixed and documented
  • Configuration file is saved
  • Random seed is recorded
  • Commit hash is recorded
  • Evaluation script is version controlled
  • Output folder structure is clear
  • Main plots and tables can be regenerated
  • Hardware and runtime constraints are documented
  • Known limitations are stated

docs.title: Minimum training workflow
docs.text:
1. define task and metrics
  2. document dataset version and split
  3. select baseline model
  4. define configuration file
  5. run controlled training
  6. evaluate on held-out data
  7. store checkpoints and logs
  8. summarize findings

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
• start with a reproducible baseline
  • change one major factor at a time
  • record failed runs when informative
  • separate exploratory work from final benchmark runs

docs.title: Data Management
docs.text: This section defines how research data should be stored, documented, versioned, backed up, and protected.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This includes: - images and videos - annotations - point clouds and meshes - models and checkpoints - scripts and configurations - logs and results - reports and derived technical outputs

docs.title: Backup principles
docs.text:
• use institutionally approved storage for canonical copies
  • maintain at least one additional backup for critical assets
  • do not keep the only copy on a laptop or external drive

docs.title: Security principles
docs.text:
• restrict access by project need
  • remove access when members leave the project
  • never publish sensitive data without approval
  • document external sharing of large datasets

docs.title: Suggested practice
docs.text:
• primary storage on managed institutional infrastructure
  • mirrored backup for critical project data
  • encrypted storage for sensitive material when applicable

docs.title: Raw
docs.text: Original unmodified data as acquired from sensors, field campaigns, collaborators, or public sources.

docs.title: Interim
docs.text: Temporary outputs produced during cleaning, conversion, or structuring.

docs.title: Processed
docs.text: Curated data ready for modeling, analysis, or publication.

docs.title: Results
docs.text: Outputs generated by analysis, training, reconstruction, or visualization pipelines.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• raw data is read-only after ingestion
  • processed data must be linked to a script or workflow that produced it
  • results should include references to the exact input data and code version

docs.title: Recommended lab-level hierarchy
docs.text:
LAB_ROOT/
├── admin/
├── shared-resources/
├── templates/
├── projects/
│   ├── project-01-name/
│   ├── project-02-name/
│   └── ...
└── archive/

docs.title: Recommended project-level hierarchy
docs.text:
project-name/
├── 00_admin/
├── 01_data_raw/
├── 02_data_interim/
├── 03_data_processed/
├── 04_annotations/
├── 05_code/
├── 06_models/
├── 07_experiments/
├── 08_results/
├── 09_reports/
└── 10_archive/

docs.title: Why numbered folders can help
docs.text: Numbered folders keep project directories ordered consistently across operating systems and file explorers.

docs.title: Metadata Requirements
docs.text: Every important dataset or technical artifact should be accompanied by metadata.

docs.title: Minimum metadata fields
docs.text:
• title
  • owner
  • creation date
  • acquisition method
  • location or source
  • file format
  • processing status
  • related script or workflow
  • license or usage restriction

docs.title: For image datasets
docs.text: Also record: - camera or sensor - resolution - capture conditions - georeferencing if available - known artifacts or quality issues

docs.title: Code versioning
docs.text: All active code should be kept under Git.

docs.title: Data versioning
docs.text:
Use one of the following depending on project scale:

  • folder-based versioning for small projects
  • release snapshots for milestone datasets
  • DVC for large datasets and ML pipelines

docs.title: Minimum versioning requirements
docs.text:
• each paper result must trace to a code commit
  • each trained model must trace to a data version and config
  • renamed files should not erase provenance

docs.title: 3D and Digitalization Pipelines
docs.text: This section documents standard workflows for 3D reconstruction, semantic understanding, and engineering-ready digital representations.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: LOD definitions should be adapted to the engineering use case.

docs.title: Recommended distinctions
docs.text:
• LOD for visualization: adequate for communication and XR review
  • LOD for documentation: suitable for traceable records of assets and damage
  • LOD for analysis: sufficiently structured for downstream mechanical or engineering interpretation

docs.title: Minimum requirement
docs.text: Every project using the term LOD must define explicitly what geometric, semantic, and condition information is included.

docs.title: Standard operations
docs.text:
• cleaning
  • outlier removal
  • hole inspection
  • simplification when justified
  • normal consistency checks
  • coordinate system documentation

docs.title: Output expectations
docs.text: Every delivered mesh should include: - file format - units - coordinate reference description - source workflow - date of generation

docs.title: Intended use
docs.text: These methods are useful for novel view synthesis, scene representation, and visualization-oriented digitalization workflows.

docs.title: Minimum reporting items
docs.text:
• input image source and count
  • camera pose source
  • model type and implementation
  • training settings
  • rendering outputs generated
  • limitations for metric or geometric use

docs.title: Important note
docs.text: State clearly when an output is intended for visualization versus geometry extraction or engineering analysis.

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text:
1. image acquisition planning
  2. quality check of images
  3. feature extraction and matching
  4. sparse reconstruction
  5. dense reconstruction
  6. meshing or point cloud processing
  7. scaling and registration
  8. quality assessment

docs.title: Recommended documentation
docs.text:
• acquisition date and device
  • overlap expectations
  • reconstruction software and version
  • alignment parameters
  • scaling method
  • known gaps or uncertainty zones

docs.title: Infrastructure and Resources
docs.text: This section documents compute, storage, and equipment resources available to the lab.

docs.title: Document for each resource
docs.text:
• provider
  • purpose
  • owner
  • billing source
  • who can request access
  • allowed workloads
  • storage and security constraints

docs.title: Suggested fields to document
docs.text:
• machine name
  • operating system
  • GPU model and count
  • storage location
  • access procedure
  • usage rules
  • maintenance contact

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Document both how to use the server and how not to misuse it.

docs.title: Suggested inventory categories
docs.text:
• XR devices
  • cameras and lenses
  • LiDAR or depth sensors
  • robots and payloads
  • workstations
  • field inspection accessories

docs.title: For each item, track
docs.text:
• ID or asset tag
  • location
  • responsible person
  • booking or borrowing rules
  • status

docs.title: Lab Manual — Overview
docs.text:
The Lab Manual serves as a central reference for all members of the DISC Laboratory. It gathers the key documents, guidelines, and practices that define how the group operates on a daily basis.

The purpose of this section is to:

  • provide clear and consistent guidance on how research activities are conducted within the lab;
  • ensure alignment across all members in terms of expectations, workflows, and standards;
  • support efficient onboarding of new members;
  • promote good practices in research, collaboration, and organization.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
The Lab Manual includes documents such as:

  • Onboarding guidelines Instructions for new members to integrate into the lab and understand the main tools, platforms, and workflows.

  • Data and project management guidelines Standards for organizing files, managing data, documenting experiments, and maintaining reproducibility.

  • Expectations documents Role-specific expectations (e.g., PhD students, visiting researchers, postdoctoral researchers) outlining responsibilities, working principles, and performance standards.

docs.title: Intention
docs.text:
The Lab Manual is not intended to be restrictive or exhaustive. Instead, it provides a structured foundation that:

  • reduces ambiguity in how work is organized and conducted;
  • facilitates collaboration and continuity across projects;
  • supports the production of high-quality, reproducible research.

Members are encouraged to consult the Lab Manual regularly and to contribute to its improvement when relevant.

docs.title: DISC Laboratory --- Data and Project Management Guidelines
docs.text: This document defines the standard structure and rules for organizing research-related materials within the Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction (DISC) Laboratory. The goal is to ensure consistency, reproducibility, traceability, and efficient collaboration across all projects and among all team members, including PhD students, postdoctoral researchers, undergraduate students, research staff, and collaborators.

docs.title: Purpose of this Document
docs.text:
This document provides the lab-wide guidelines for storing, managing, and documenting project information. It is intended to help all members of the group understand where files should be placed, how project materials should be organized, and what minimum documentation standards are expected.

These guidelines are meant to support good research practice. They do not replace university, sponsor, data protection, safety, ethics, or other institutional requirements. Where relevant, official NTU rules and project-specific conditions remain controlling.

docs.title: Project-centric organization
docs.text: All research materials must be organized by project, not by individual person. Projects often continue even when students or staff leave the group, and multiple members may contribute to the same project over time. For this reason, all project data, code, reports, and outputs must remain within the corresponding project folder.

docs.title: Data integrity
docs.text: Raw data is considered the original record of acquisition and must never be modified, overwritten, or deleted casually. Any cleaning, transformation, annotation, or reformatting must be carried out on copies of the data stored in the appropriate processed-data folders.

docs.title: Version control
docs.text: All core code must be managed through the official GitHub organization of the laboratory. Local folders may contain clones of repositories, temporary scripts, or exploratory notebooks, but important code must not exist only on a local machine.

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text: Experiments must be documented well enough that another group member can understand what was done, what data was used, what configuration was run, and how the outputs were generated.

docs.title: Controlled access
docs.text: Where relevant, folder permissions should be used to protect sensitive or critical content. In particular, raw data folders should be treated as read-only after validation, and only authorized members should modify official project material.

docs.title: Global Lab Folder Structure
docs.text:
The overall lab storage should follow a structure similar to the one below:

DISC_Lab/
|
|-- 00_Admin/
|-- 01_Lab_Manual/
|-- 02_Projects/
|-- 03_Publications/
|-- 04_Templates/

docs.title: 00_Admin/
docs.text:
This folder contains administrative and operational documents related to the management of the lab.

Typical content includes:

  • budgets and financial tracking;
  • hiring and recruitment documents;
  • meeting notes for lab or PI meetings;
  • internal reports;
  • grant tracking and administrative records.

This folder is intended for operational purposes only. Research data and project-specific technical materials should not be stored here.

docs.title: 01_Lab_Manual/
docs.text:
This folder contains the guidelines, standards, and procedures that define how research should be conducted and documented in the lab.

Typical content includes:

  • data management guidelines;
  • coding standards;
  • experiment tracking guidelines;
  • GitHub and version control workflows;
  • onboarding instructions.

These documents define how work should be done in the lab. All lab members are expected to follow them. Project-specific files should not be stored here.

docs.title: 02_Projects/
docs.text:
This folder contains all active research projects in the lab. It is the core working area of the lab, and all research activities must be organized here by project rather than by person.

Each project must follow the standard structure:

PROJ_XXX_ProjectName/
|-- 00_Admin/
|-- 01_Data/
|-- 02_Code/
|-- 03_Models/
|-- 04_Experiments/
|-- 05_Results/
|-- 06_Reports/
|-- 07_Presentations/
|-- 08_Documents/
|-- 09_Archive/

All work must be organized by project, not by individual. Members should follow the structure strictly and refer to the lab manual for more detailed procedural guidance.

docs.title: 03_Publications/
docs.text:
This folder contains all materials related to publications.

Typical content includes:

  • journal papers;
  • conference papers;
  • submitted manuscripts;
  • supplementary materials;
  • figures and tables prepared for publications.

A suggested structure is:

Paper_Name/
|-- manuscript/
|-- figures/
|-- tables/
|-- supplementary/
|-- submission/

Final versions should be clearly identified, and publication materials should remain consistent with the corresponding project results.

docs.title: 04_Templates/
docs.text:
This folder contains reusable templates for the lab.

Typical content includes:

  • LaTeX templates for papers, reports, and CVs;
  • presentation templates;
  • report templates;
  • code templates and utility scripts;
  • experiment logging templates.

These files should be used as starting points. Members should create copies when needed rather than modifying the master templates directly.

docs.title: Mandatory Project Folder Structure
docs.text:
Every project must follow the same internal structure. A standard project folder should look like this:

PROJ_XXX_ProjectName/
|
|-- 00_Admin/
|-- 01_Data/
|-- 02_Code/
|-- 03_Models/
|-- 04_Experiments/
|-- 05_Results/
|-- 06_Reports/
|-- 07_Presentations/
|-- 08_Documents/
|-- 09_Archive/

docs.title: Project naming convention
docs.text:
Each project folder should be named using the format:

PROJ_XXX_ProjectName

where XXX is a three-digit identifier and ProjectName is a short descriptive name written with underscores instead of spaces when needed.

Examples:

  • PROJ_001_XR_Bridge_Inspection
  • PROJ_002_Heritage_Digitalization
  • PROJ_003_Robotic_Assembly

docs.title: Detailed Description of Each Project Folder
00_Admin/
docs.text:
This folder stores project-specific administrative and management material.

Typical content includes:

  • meeting notes;
  • project timelines and milestone tracking;
  • internal task assignments;
  • communication summaries relevant to the project;
  • staffing or role definitions specific to the project.

This folder should not be used to store research outputs, datasets, or technical code.

docs.title: 01_Data/
docs.text:
This is one of the most important folders in the project. It contains all project-related datasets and associated metadata.

The recommended internal structure is:

01_Data/
|-- 00_Raw/
|-- 01_Processed/
|-- 02_Annotations/
|-- 03_External/
|-- 04_Splits/
|-- README.md

docs.title: 00_Raw/
docs.text:
This folder contains original acquired data, such as images, videos, point clouds, LiDAR scans, sensor readings, or any other primary data source. Once uploaded and verified, this folder should be treated as read-only.

Rules:

  • never modify raw files directly;
  • never overwrite raw files with processed versions;
  • never store edited files in the raw-data folder.

docs.title: 01_Processed/
docs.text: This folder contains cleaned, transformed, converted, or otherwise processed versions of the raw data. Any scripts or notes that explain the processing pipeline should be linked clearly to these files.

docs.title: 02_Annotations/
docs.text: This folder stores labels, masks, segmentation maps, bounding boxes, metadata tables, and exports from annotation tools. Annotation guidelines, class names, labeling conventions, and format definitions should also be documented here or in a dedicated README file.

docs.title: 03_External/
docs.text: This folder contains public or externally sourced datasets used in the project. It should be clear what dataset was used, from where it was obtained, and under what version or release.

docs.title: 04_Splits/
docs.text: This folder stores train, validation, and test splits, or other project-specific partitions of the data. These files are important for reproducibility and should be versioned where necessary.

docs.title: README requirements for the data folder
docs.text:
The README.md within the data folder should describe, at minimum:

  • the origin of the data;
  • acquisition date or campaign;
  • sensors or devices used;
  • preprocessing steps;
  • data format;
  • naming conventions;
  • known limitations or exclusions.

docs.title: 02_Code/
docs.text:
This folder stores the coding material associated with the project. The recommended structure is:

02_Code/
|-- main_repo/
|-- experiments/
|-- notebooks/
|-- README.md

docs.title: main_repo/
docs.text: This should contain a clone of the official project repository hosted in the DISC Lab GitHub organization. This is the main location for the active codebase.

docs.title: experiments/
docs.text: This folder may contain temporary scripts, local tests, or quick development material that is not yet mature enough to become part of the main repository. Useful scripts should eventually be cleaned and migrated into the main repository if they become part of the project workflow.

docs.title: notebooks/
docs.text: This folder is intended for Jupyter notebooks or other exploratory computational documents used for analysis, visualization, or quick prototyping.

docs.title: Rules for the code folder
docs.text:
• important code must not be stored only locally;
  • use meaningful commit messages in GitHub;
  • document dependencies and environment setup;
  • remove or archive obsolete scripts when they are no longer relevant.

docs.title: 03_Models/
docs.text:
This folder is intended for machine learning or computational model artifacts. A recommended structure is:

03_Models/
|-- checkpoints/
|-- final_models/
|-- configs/
|-- logs/

docs.title: checkpoints/
docs.text: Intermediate training states or periodic saved model states.

docs.title: final_models/
docs.text: Final selected models used for analysis, deployment, reporting, or publication.

docs.title: configs/
docs.text: Configuration files used for training, inference, evaluation, or deployment.

docs.title: logs/
docs.text: Training logs, run summaries, metric outputs, and related records.

docs.title: Rules for the models folder
docs.text:
Every stored model should be traceable to:

  • the dataset version used;
  • the code version or commit;
  • the configuration file used;
  • the corresponding experiment folder if applicable.

docs.title: 04_Experiments/
docs.text:
This folder is central to the scientific quality and traceability of the project. Every experiment should have its own folder.

Example:

04_Experiments/
|-- EXP_001_initial_training/
    |-- config.yaml
    |-- notes.md
    |-- outputs/

Each experiment folder should include at least:

  • a configuration file or parameter record;
  • a short notes file explaining the objective of the experiment;
  • a description of what changed with respect to previous runs;
  • references to the dataset version and model version used;
  • the outputs generated by that run.

Experiments should be named systematically, for example:

EXP_001_initial_training

docs.title: 05_Results/
docs.text:
This folder stores the outputs that are used in papers, reports, presentations, or discussions.

A suggested structure is:

05_Results/
|-- figures/
|-- tables/
|-- videos/
|-- qualitative/

Typical content includes:

  • publication-ready figures;
  • performance tables;
  • visualization videos;
  • qualitative comparisons and inspection outputs.

This folder should contain organized outputs, not temporary scratch files.

docs.title: 06_Reports/
docs.text:
This folder stores written project outputs and internal reporting documents.

A suggested structure is:

06_Reports/
|-- internal/
|-- milestones/
|-- thesis/

It may include:

  • internal progress reports;
  • sponsor deliverables;
  • milestone summaries;
  • thesis reports, drafts, or appendices when relevant.

docs.title: 07_Presentations/
docs.text:
This folder stores all slides and presentation files associated with the project.

Examples include:

  • group meeting presentations;
  • progress review slides;
  • conference presentations;
  • defense presentations.

Files should be named clearly, for example 20260415_group_update.pdf.

docs.title: 08_Documents/
docs.text:
This folder stores formal non-report documentation associated with the project.

A suggested structure is:

08_Documents/
|-- proposals/
|-- agreements/
|-- ethics/

Typical content includes:

  • submitted proposals and proposal drafts;
  • collaboration agreements or memoranda;
  • ethics approvals or ethics-related submissions;
  • formal documentation required by the institution or sponsor.

docs.title: 09_Archive/
docs.text:
This folder stores deprecated, inactive, or superseded materials that should be retained for record purposes but should no longer be used as active working files.

Examples include:

  • old report versions;
  • outdated figures or tables;
  • scripts replaced by newer workflows;
  • unused supporting files from earlier phases of the project.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Consistent naming is required to avoid confusion and improve searchability.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
Use:

PROJ_XXX_ProjectName

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
Use:

EXP_XXX_Description

docs.title: Dataset versions
docs.text:
Use clear version labels, for example:

  • dataset_v1
  • dataset_v2

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
Whenever appropriate, use date-first naming in the form:

YYYYMMDD_description.ext

This convention is recommended especially for reports, presentation files, and meeting-related documents.

docs.title: Principal Investigator
docs.text: The PI is responsible for overseeing compliance with the general structure, defining permissions where needed, and ensuring that the overall project organization supports continuity, integrity, and accessibility.

docs.title: Researchers and students
docs.text:
All members of the lab are responsible for:

  • following the folder structure consistently;
  • documenting experiments and datasets properly;
  • storing work in the appropriate project location;
  • using version control for code;
  • avoiding private or isolated storage of important project materials.

docs.title: GitHub Usage
docs.text:
All core code developed in the lab should be stored in the DISC Lab GitHub organization.

Recommended practice includes:

  • one repository per project, tool, or sufficiently independent codebase;
  • no large raw datasets in GitHub;
  • no unnecessary storage of large trained models in GitHub unless specifically justified;
  • use of GitHub for version control, collaboration, issue tracking, and documentation.

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
The quality of research outputs depends not only on the scientific ideas and technical work, but also on the clarity and discipline with which information is managed. A well-organized project structure reduces errors, facilitates continuity when group members change, and makes it much easier to reproduce results, prepare publications, and respond to sponsor or institutional requirements.

When in doubt, the default rule is simple: place files in the project folder, document what was done, avoid modifying raw data, and keep important code and experimental records traceable.

Maintained by: DISC Laboratory Last updated: March 27, 2026

docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Welcome to the Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction (DISC) Laboratory.

We are excited to have you join the group. This document will guide you through your first days and help you align with how we work, collaborate, and conduct research.

This is not meant to be exhaustive — you will learn many things progressively — but it provides the key elements to start effectively.

docs.title: First Day Overview
docs.text: During the first day, the following topics are typically covered:

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Overview of the lab research vision and ongoing projects;
  • Discussion of the researcher’s role and expected contributions;
  • Introduction to collaborators and current work;
  • Setup of required accounts and platforms.

docs.title: Accounts and platforms
docs.text:
Please ensure you have access to:

  • GitHub accounts
  • OrcId account
  • ResearchGate account
  • Google scholar account
  • LinkedIn account (recommended)

docs.title: Lab Website
docs.text:
The lab website contains all relevan information related to the DISCLab research group. This includes the groups research vision, research areas, team members, publications, news and positions available.

Each member will be added to the lab website with:

  • a profile photo;
  • contact info, a short research description or interests.

docs.title: GitHub (DISC Lab)
docs.text:
All code development is managed through the lab GitHub organization.

  • Version control must be used consistently;
  • Code should be pushed regularly;
  • Repositories should be clean and documented.

docs.title: DISCLab wiki
docs.text: The Lab's Wiki page contain relevant information useful for the team members. It is encoraged to review the content as well to contribute to it. The objective of this page is to have a central knowledge base of the group for any possible operational aspect. This involves guideliness, templates, tutorials, best practices, etc.

docs.title: Youtube
docs.text:
YouTube channel is used to share videos related to research methodologies developed in the group.

  • It is encouraged to prepare a short video presenting the methodologies and showcasing case of studies;
  • Do this for journal or conference papers.

docs.title: LinkedIn
docs.text:
LinkedIn page is used to communicate the group's research activities.

  • Members are encouraged to maintain a profile;
  • Participation is optional but recommended.

docs.title: ResearchGate
docs.text:
ResearcgGate page is used to dissiminate the group's research publications.

  • Once a paper is published, a researchgate post is expected;
  • Provide the full paper (if not possible due to copyright, publish the latest submited manuscript) together with figures.

docs.title: Profile Information
docs.text:
New members are requested to provide:

  • a natural professional photo (no heavy editing);
  • a short research bio (3--5 lines);
  • current affiliation and role.

This will be used for:

  • Lab website
  • Internal records
  • Research visibility

docs.title: Working Structure
docs.text:
All research activities are organized using a project-based structure.

  • The main workspace is located in 02_Projects/;
  • Each project follows a standardized internal structure;

Each project contains its own README files explaining how to organize work.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
• Core code must be stored in GitHub;
  • Important code should not remain only locally;
  • Code should be clear and maintainable.

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
• Experiments must be documented;
  • Keep track of: configurations, datasets, and results (should be traceable).

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
• Raw data must not be modified;
  • Data structure must be followed consistently.

docs.title: Research Materials
docs.text:
You will receive:

  • Relevant project documents (proposals, notes, datasets)
  • Access to existing code repositories
  • References to relevant literature

docs.title: Meetings
docs.text:
• Regular meetings will be scheduled (weakly: individual and group);

docs.title: Expectations

docs.title: Available Resources

docs.title: First Week Goals

By the end of the first week, members should:

docs.title: Expectations — Overview
docs.text:
This section outlines the main expectations for members of the DISC Laboratory. These documents are intended to provide clarity on the professional standards, working principles, and research practices expected within the group.

The goal is not to create unnecessary rigidity, but to establish a shared understanding of how research should be conducted, how responsibilities should be handled, and how members are expected to contribute to a productive, respectful, and well-organized research environment.

The expectations documents complement the onboarding and data management guidelines. Together, they help define not only what work is done in the lab, but also how that work should be approached.

docs.title: What these documents cover
docs.text:
The expectations documents typically address topics such as:

  • professionalism, communication, and reliability;
  • ownership of work and research initiative;
  • quality standards in experiments, code, and documentation;
  • collaboration, authorship, and team conduct;
  • reporting, continuity, and responsible management of research outputs.

docs.title: Core expectations
docs.text:
Across all roles, some core expectations remain the same:

  • communicate early when blocked;
  • document your work continuously;
  • maintain your repositories and files cleanly;
  • be reliable with deadlines and meetings;
  • ask questions after making a serious first attempt.

docs.title: Professional expectation
docs.text:
Good research practice includes good documentation, not only good technical output.

Clear communication, organized work, and responsible collaboration are considered essential parts of research quality in the lab.

docs.title: PhD Student Expectations and Working Principles
docs.text: This document outlines the main expectations, working norms, and professional responsibilities associated with a PhD appointment in the **Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction research group at NTU Singapore. It is intended to promote transparency, alignment, and a productive working relationship from the outset.

docs.title: Purpose of this Document
docs.text:
This document is provided at the beginning of the PhD to clarify expectations early in the doctoral journey. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, support mutual understanding, and help prevent avoidable misunderstandings at a later stage.

This document is not an employment contract and does not replace university, school, scholarship, funding-agency, immigration, human resources, safety, ethics, or other institutional rules. Official NTU policies, doctoral program requirements, and the terms of the formal appointment remain controlling where applicable. Instead, this document sets out the research-group-level expectations that the PhD student is expected to understand and accept before or at the start of joining the group.

docs.title: Nature of the Appointment
docs.text:
A PhD appointment in the group is, above all, a research training process aimed at developing an independent researcher. It is not simply a role for executing predefined tasks, and it is also not expected to begin at the same level of independence or maturity as a postdoctoral appointment. Rather, the PhD is expected to evolve over time from guided learning to increasing intellectual ownership, sound judgement, accountability, and the ability to contribute meaningfully to the scientific direction and visibility of the research.

The PhD student is expected to:

  • develop progressively from a trainee into an increasingly independent researcher;
  • contribute actively to the research objectives of the funded project(s) and the broader mission of the group;
  • work with professionalism, integrity, consistency, and collegiality;
  • make steady progress toward high-quality scholarly outputs and a coherent doctoral thesis;
  • help maintain an effective, respectful, and well-organized research environment;
  • progressively build the habits, standards, and judgement expected of a future independent academic or research professional.

The student is not expected to operate from day one at the level of a postdoctoral researcher. However, the expectation is that independence, technical depth, scientific judgement, and ownership of the work should increase significantly over the course of the PhD.

docs.title: General Professional Expectations
docs.text:
The PhD student is expected to:

  • behave professionally and respectfully toward all students, staff, collaborators, and administrative personnel;
  • communicate in a timely, clear, and constructive manner;
  • act with honesty in research, reporting, authorship, data handling, and use of resources;
  • respect deadlines, commitments, and agreed responsibilities;
  • raise concerns early rather than allowing issues to accumulate;
  • represent the group and institution professionally in meetings, presentations, conferences, site visits, and online communication;
  • comply with relevant university and laboratory rules, including those related to safety, ethics, confidentiality, and responsible conduct of research.

A PhD is a training process, but this does not reduce the expectation of professionalism. Learning and development are expected to occur within a framework of seriousness, accountability, and respect for others.

docs.title: Ownership and initiative
docs.text:
The PhD student is expected to take increasing ownership of the assigned research agenda over time. In the early stage of the PhD, this may involve more guidance, more frequent discussion, and more emphasis on learning established tools, methods, and literature. As the PhD progresses, the student is expected to become progressively more capable of identifying technical bottlenecks, proposing solutions, planning next steps, and moving the work forward without requiring excessive day-to-day direction.

The PhD student should progressively learn to:

  • understand the scientific goals, technical scope, and deliverables of the project;
  • plan and execute research activities with an increasing degree of independence;
  • troubleshoot problems systematically and document solutions;
  • critically assess results rather than only generating them;
  • propose new ideas, extensions, and improvements where relevant;
  • connect day-to-day technical work to broader scientific questions and thesis-level contributions.

A PhD student is not expected to start with full independence. However, a continued pattern of passivity, excessive dependence, or lack of intellectual engagement over a prolonged period will be a serious concern. Growth toward independence is a central expectation of the doctoral process.

docs.title: Quality of work
docs.text:
The group places strong emphasis on rigor and quality. The PhD student is expected to produce work that is scientifically sound, technically careful, reproducible where relevant, and presented to a professional academic standard appropriate to the student's stage of development.

This includes expectations such as:

  • careful experimental design and documentation;
  • appropriate validation, benchmarking, and uncertainty awareness;
  • clean, understandable, and well-documented code and computational workflows;
  • organized data and version control practices;
  • accurate and thoughtful interpretation of results;
  • professional writing for manuscripts, reports, proposals, and presentations, with progressive improvement over time.

At the beginning of the PhD, not every aspect of the work is expected to be at a mature level. Nevertheless, the student is expected to take quality seriously from the outset and to show a clear willingness to improve through feedback and iteration.

docs.title: Productivity and deliverables
docs.text:
The precise expected outputs depend on the project, scholarship or funding conditions, stage of the PhD, and the nature of the research. However, the PhD student is generally expected to contribute meaningfully over the course of the doctorate to several of the following:

  • journal papers and conference papers;
  • technical reports, datasets, codebases, prototypes, or demonstrators;
  • internal project milestones and sponsor deliverables where relevant;
  • literature reviews, methodological studies, and validation work;
  • presentations to collaborators, sponsors, or the research community;
  • the development of a coherent doctoral thesis.

Publication quantity alone is not the only performance measure, particularly in the earlier stages of the PhD. However, a sustained lack of tangible research output, without justified reasons and prior discussion, will be considered a serious concern. The student is expected to make steady progress and to convert research effort into concrete outcomes over time.

docs.title: Stage-specific expectations across the PhD
docs.text:
The balance between learning, execution, originality, and independence is expected to evolve during the PhD.

In the **early stage of the PhD, the student is generally expected to:

  • build a solid understanding of the research area and relevant literature;
  • become familiar with the lab's tools, datasets, workflows, and research standards;
  • reproduce baselines, understand existing pipelines, and learn to work rigorously;
  • identify promising directions with guidance from the supervisor.

In the **intermediate stage of the PhD, the student is generally expected to:

  • take more ownership of technical development and experimentation;
  • contribute original ideas, methodological improvements, or new integrations;
  • produce increasingly mature research outputs and contribute to publications;
  • demonstrate clearer scientific judgement and more independent planning.

In the **advanced stage of the PhD, the student is generally expected to:

  • lead major parts of the research direction within the scope of the thesis;
  • articulate clearly the novelty and significance of the doctoral contributions;
  • produce high-quality publications and a coherent thesis narrative;
  • support the continuity of the work and, where appropriate, help mentor more junior members.

These stages are indicative rather than rigid, since progress rates vary across students and projects. The important expectation is a credible trajectory of development.

docs.title: Communication, Meetings, and Responsiveness
docs.text:
The PhD student is expected to maintain regular communication with the supervisor and relevant team members.

Minimum expectations include:

  • attending scheduled one-to-one meetings, group meetings, project meetings, and other essential research discussions;
  • preparing adequately for meetings, including bringing updates, questions, and next steps;
  • responding to important emails and messages within a reasonable time frame;
  • informing the supervisor in advance when deadlines may not be met;
  • communicating absences, delays, obstacles, or personal constraints that materially affect project progress;
  • sharing information in a timely way rather than working in isolation on issues that affect the team.

As a general working norm, communication should be proactive. Repeated silence, lack of responsiveness, or lack of visibility over ongoing work is not acceptable in a collaborative research environment. For a PhD student, this is especially important because regular communication is a key part of the training process.

docs.title: Working Style, Presence, and Time Commitment
docs.text:
A PhD is an outcomes-driven research training process rather than a purely time-clocked role. At the same time, the PhD student is expected to demonstrate a professional level of presence, engagement, and reliability.

The PhD student is expected to:

  • maintain an appropriate and consistent level of engagement with the project and group;
  • be reasonably available during normal working periods for meetings and collaborative work;
  • spend sufficient time on the research to meet expected progress and doctoral milestones;
  • use flexible working arrangements responsibly, where permitted;
  • discuss in advance any recurring schedule constraints, remote-working plans, travel, or other factors that may affect availability.

A PhD should not be treated as a passive enrollment status or as a role with minimal engagement. The level of commitment should reflect the seriousness of doctoral research training and the expectations associated with funded academic work.

docs.title: Reporting and Documentation
docs.text:
Good research management requires proper documentation. The PhD student is expected to maintain orderly records of relevant work, which may include:

  • experiment logs and methodological notes;
  • code repositories and version histories;
  • structured storage of datasets, models, scripts, figures, and reports;
  • summaries of results, decisions, and action items;
  • progress updates suitable for internal tracking, annual reviews, milestone assessments, or sponsor reporting where relevant.

If requested, the PhD student should provide concise periodic written updates on progress, obstacles, priorities, and next steps. Research materials generated within the PhD should be stored in a manner accessible to the group and not only on personal devices or private accounts.

Good documentation is not only an administrative requirement. It is also part of the student's scientific development and is essential for reproducibility, continuity, and thesis preparation.

docs.title: Publication culture
docs.text:
The group values high-quality scholarly publication and responsible dissemination. The PhD student is expected to contribute actively to manuscript preparation, revision, and submission when publication is an appropriate output of the work.

Because publication is an important part of doctoral development, the student is expected to treat writing as a core research activity rather than as a secondary task postponed until the end of the PhD.

docs.title: Authorship principles
docs.text:
Authorship should reflect substantial intellectual or technical contribution and should follow accepted academic norms as well as any relevant institutional or journal guidance. Authorship order will depend on the nature and extent of contributions and will be discussed in good faith.

General expectations include:

  • authorship is earned through substantial contribution, not assumed by title or seniority alone;
  • first authorship typically requires a leading contribution to the core research and manuscript preparation;
  • for publications arising primarily from the group's projects, funding, infrastructure, supervision, or intellectual direction, the supervisor must be listed as the last author, serve as the corresponding author, and be acknowledged as the principal investigator;
  • for other publications connected to work conducted within the group, authorship and author order should be discussed with the supervisor at an early stage and determined based on the nature and extent of contributions;
  • all listed authors are expected to contribute, review, and stand by the submitted work to a reasonable extent;
  • acknowledgement may be more appropriate than authorship for limited or peripheral contributions.

The PhD student should not submit manuscripts, abstracts, or related outputs based on group work without prior discussion and approval from the supervisor and relevant co-authors.

docs.title: Affiliation and acknowledgement
docs.text: All outputs arising from the PhD are expected to include the correct institutional affiliation(s), required funding acknowledgements, and other sponsor- or project-specific statements where applicable.

docs.title: Data, Code, Intellectual Property, and Confidentiality
Data and code management
docs.text: Research data, code, models, annotations, figures, notes, and related materials produced as part of the PhD must be handled responsibly and organized professionally. The PhD student is expected to follow agreed project conventions for storage, naming, backup, version control, and documentation.

docs.title: Ownership and access
docs.text: Materials generated within the scope of the PhD, especially where tied to funded projects, collaborations, group infrastructure, or institutional resources, should be treated as project or institutional research assets rather than purely personal assets. The PhD student must not withhold essential files, repositories, documentation, or access credentials needed for continuity of the research.

docs.title: Confidentiality
docs.text: The PhD student may have access to unpublished results, confidential data, sponsor-sensitive information, reviewer discussions, internal proposals, software, or collaboration materials. Such information must not be disclosed, shared externally, or reused inappropriately without authorization.

docs.title: Intellectual property and external use
docs.text: Any potentially patentable, commercially sensitive, or otherwise protected outputs should be discussed before public disclosure. The PhD student should not independently commercialize, license, distribute, or externally reuse project materials in ways that conflict with university policy, sponsor terms, collaboration agreements, or the legitimate interests of the group.

docs.title: Collaboration and Team Conduct
docs.text:
The group values a constructive, respectful, and collaborative culture. The PhD student is expected to contribute positively to that environment.

This includes:

  • interacting respectfully with students and colleagues;
  • giving and receiving technically sound and professional feedback;
  • avoiding dismissive, hostile, or inappropriate behavior;
  • recognizing that collaboration requires transparency and mutual accountability;
  • supporting a culture in which members can ask questions and seek guidance without fear of disrespect.

As the PhD progresses, the student is also expected to become a constructive member of the lab community and, where appropriate, to contribute to a positive research culture for newer members.

docs.title: Mentoring and Support of Junior Researchers
docs.text:
Depending on project needs and stage of development, the PhD student may be asked to support the technical mentoring of master students, undergraduate students, visiting students, interns, or research assistants. This may include onboarding, providing technical guidance, reviewing code or manuscripts, helping design experiments, or advising on good research practice.

Such mentoring is part of research development when relevant and should be done responsibly. However, the primary focus of the PhD student remains their own doctoral development and thesis progress. Any mentoring role should therefore be appropriate to the student's stage and should not displace the student's core research responsibilities.

docs.title: Funding, Resources, Travel, and Expenses
docs.text:
Access to equipment, software, computing resources, travel, consumables, or project-related expenses is subject to budget availability, institutional rules, project scope, and supervisory approval.

The PhD student is expected to:

  • use research funds and resources responsibly;
  • seek approval before incurring reimbursable expenses where required;
  • align conference and travel plans with project priorities, doctoral stage, and available resources;
  • understand that not all conferences, travel requests, or purchases can necessarily be supported;
  • handle institutional assets with care.

Travel and conference participation are valued, but they should be aligned with research readiness, available budget, and the broader priorities of the group and project.

docs.title: Compliance, Ethics, and Safety
docs.text:
The PhD student is expected to comply with all applicable requirements related to research integrity, human subjects, data governance, safety training, laboratory or workshop rules, fieldwork procedures, and any project-specific ethical or regulatory approvals.

No research activity requiring prior approval should begin before the relevant approvals are in place.

docs.title: Communication of Concerns and Resolution of Issues
docs.text:
A productive research environment requires open, respectful, and timely communication in both directions. The PhD student is encouraged to raise concerns early whenever a problem, misunderstanding, or source of friction arises that may affect the research, working relationships, or the overall working environment.

Examples of concerns that should be communicated early include, but are not limited to:

  • misunderstandings regarding responsibilities, expectations, or priorities;
  • difficulties in communication or collaboration with other group members, students, staff, or external collaborators;
  • concerns about authorship, acknowledgement, contribution, or publication planning;
  • disagreement regarding research direction, technical decisions, or allocation of effort;
  • obstacles affecting progress, including lack of clarity, resource limitations, or personal circumstances that materially affect the work;
  • any situation in which an issue may worsen if left unaddressed.

In most cases, concerns should first be raised with the supervisor in a professional and constructive manner. The purpose of such discussion is to clarify expectations, understand the issue, and identify a reasonable path forward. Where appropriate, concerns involving other members of the group or collaborators may also be addressed through a joint discussion with the relevant parties.

Concerns raised in good faith will be treated seriously and respectfully. Open communication is considered an important part of maintaining a healthy, fair, and productive research environment.

docs.title: Performance Concerns and Escalation
docs.text:
If concerns arise regarding performance, conduct, communication, professionalism, fulfilment of responsibilities, or credible progress toward doctoral development, the usual approach will be to discuss them directly and in good faith at an early stage. Depending on the issue, expectations may be clarified verbally or in writing, and reasonable time may be given for improvement where appropriate.

Examples of issues that may require attention include, but are not limited to:

  • persistent lack of progress without reasonable explanation;
  • repeated failure to meet agreed responsibilities or deadlines;
  • poor communication or prolonged unresponsiveness;
  • failure to document or share essential research materials appropriately;
  • serious lapses in professional conduct or research integrity;
  • behaviour that negatively affects the working environment or project continuity;
  • prolonged lack of development toward the expected level of doctoral independence.

Nothing in this document limits the application of formal institutional procedures where required.

docs.title: End-of-PhD Responsibilities
docs.text:
Before the PhD ends, the student is expected to support an orderly transition. This includes, as applicable:

  • organizing and transferring project files, repositories, data, models, and documentation;
  • summarizing the status of ongoing tasks, manuscripts, and deliverables;
  • returning equipment, access items, or other institutional property as required;
  • clarifying any remaining publication, authorship, or handover matters;
  • helping ensure that the doctoral work is archived in a way that remains understandable and usable by the group.

Completion of the PhD should not leave the project in a state where critical knowledge or materials are inaccessible.

docs.title: Mutual Understanding
docs.text: The goal of this document is not to create rigidity, but to set a professional foundation for an effective doctoral journey. The supervisor is committed to providing academic guidance, regular feedback, reasonable access to discussion, and support for the student's scientific and professional growth. In return, the PhD student is expected to engage seriously with the role, take increasing ownership of the research over time, and uphold the standards outlined above.

docs.title: Visiting PhD Student Expectations and Working Principles
docs.text: This document outlines the main expectations, working norms, and professional responsibilities associated with a visiting PhD appointment in the **Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction research group at NTU Singapore. It is intended to promote transparency, alignment, and a productive working relationship from the outset.

docs.title: Purpose of this Document
docs.text:
This document is provided before or at the beginning of the research visit to clarify expectations early and to support an effective and productive stay in the group. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, support mutual understanding, and help prevent avoidable misunderstandings during a relatively short and focused research period.

This document is not an employment contract and does not replace university, school, scholarship, funding-agency, immigration, human resources, safety, ethics, or other institutional rules applicable to the visit. Official NTU policies, the rules of the home institution, and the formal terms of the visiting arrangement remain controlling where applicable. Instead, this document sets out the research-group-level expectations that the visiting PhD student is expected to understand and accept before or at the start of joining the group.

docs.title: Nature of the Appointment
docs.text:
A visiting PhD appointment in the group is a research-focused, time-bounded academic engagement. In most cases, the visiting student is expected to be in a relatively advanced stage of the PhD, typically around the third or fourth year, and therefore already possesses a meaningful level of technical and scientific maturity. For this reason, the visiting appointment is not expected to function in the same way as the early years of a full PhD. The emphasis is less on broad initial training and more on focused contribution, integration into ongoing work, and the development of specific outputs within a limited period.

The visiting PhD student is expected to:

  • contribute actively to the research objectives associated with the visit and, where relevant, to the broader mission of the group;
  • integrate efficiently into the scientific and technical workflow of the lab;
  • work with professionalism, integrity, consistency, and collegiality;
  • operate with a meaningful degree of independence appropriate to an advanced doctoral researcher;
  • make steady progress toward high-quality scholarly or technical outputs during the visit;
  • help maintain an effective, respectful, and well-organized research environment;
  • use the visit productively as an opportunity to deepen technical expertise, strengthen scientific judgement, and contribute to work of lasting value.

The visiting student is not expected to operate at exactly the same level of independence as a postdoctoral researcher. However, because the visit is limited in duration and typically involves a student who is already relatively advanced, the expectation is that the visiting student should be able to contribute more quickly, more autonomously, and more concretely than an early-stage PhD student.

docs.title: General Professional Expectations
docs.text:
The visiting PhD student is expected to:

  • behave professionally and respectfully toward all students, staff, collaborators, and administrative personnel;
  • communicate in a timely, clear, and constructive manner;
  • act with honesty in research, reporting, authorship, data handling, and use of resources;
  • respect deadlines, commitments, and agreed responsibilities;
  • raise concerns early rather than allowing issues to accumulate;
  • represent the group and institution professionally in meetings, presentations, conferences, site visits, and online communication;
  • comply with relevant university and laboratory rules, including those related to safety, ethics, confidentiality, and responsible conduct of research.

A visiting appointment is temporary, but the same expectation of professionalism applies. Limited duration is not a reason for reduced seriousness, reduced accountability, or informal handling of work. The student is expected to contribute responsibly and to engage with the group in a way that is respectful, reliable, and professionally appropriate.

docs.title: Ownership and initiative
docs.text:
The visiting PhD student is expected to take meaningful ownership of the assigned research agenda early in the visit. Because the visit is short, it is generally not realistic to spend an extended period only becoming familiar with the work before contributing. The student is expected to understand the scientific goals, technical scope, and intended outputs of the visit relatively quickly and to begin contributing in a focused and proactive manner.

The visiting PhD student should be able to:

  • understand the scientific goals, technical scope, and deliverables associated with the visit;
  • plan and execute research activities with a reasonable degree of independence;
  • troubleshoot problems systematically and document solutions;
  • critically assess results rather than only generating them;
  • propose technically sound ideas, extensions, and improvements where relevant;
  • connect the work carried out during the visit to broader research questions and, where applicable, to the student's home PhD trajectory.

Although guidance and discussion will of course be provided, the visiting student is expected to avoid passivity and excessive dependence. A visiting appointment works best when the student shows initiative, asks focused questions, takes responsibility for progress, and is able to convert discussion into concrete action.

docs.title: Quality of work
docs.text:
The group places strong emphasis on rigor and quality. The visiting PhD student is expected to produce work that is scientifically sound, technically careful, reproducible where relevant, and presented to a professional academic standard.

This includes expectations such as:

  • careful experimental design and documentation;
  • appropriate validation, benchmarking, and uncertainty awareness;
  • clean, understandable, and well-documented code and computational workflows;
  • organized data and version control practices;
  • accurate and thoughtful interpretation of results;
  • professional writing for manuscripts, reports, proposals, and presentations.

Because the visiting student is usually at a relatively advanced stage of doctoral training, the expectation is that a reasonable foundation in good research practice already exists. The visit may certainly involve learning new tools or approaches, but the overall standard of work is expected to be mature enough to support meaningful research contribution within the visit period.

docs.title: Productivity and deliverables
docs.text:
The precise expected outputs depend on the project, the duration of the visit, the student's background, the stage of the work, and any agreements with the home institution or funding body. However, the visiting PhD student is generally expected to contribute meaningfully during the visit to several of the following:

  • journal papers and conference papers;
  • technical reports, datasets, codebases, prototypes, or demonstrators;
  • internal project milestones and sponsor deliverables where relevant;
  • methodological studies, validation work, or integration tasks;
  • presentations to collaborators, sponsors, or the research community;
  • a clearly identifiable contribution that remains useful after the visit ends.

Publication quantity alone is not the only performance measure. However, because the visit is time-limited, a sustained lack of tangible contribution, without justified reasons and prior discussion, will be considered a serious concern. The expectation is that the student will make credible, visible progress and translate effort into concrete outcomes within the visit period.

docs.title: Stage-specific expectations across the visit
docs.text:
The visiting appointment is short and therefore should be treated as a focused research period rather than a broad exploratory phase. While every visit is different, the expected trajectory generally follows three broad stages.

In the **initial stage of the visit, the student is generally expected to:

  • become familiar quickly with the relevant project context, datasets, tools, workflows, and literature;
  • clarify the concrete goals, scope, and expected outputs of the visit;
  • identify the immediate technical tasks required to become operational;
  • begin contributing without unnecessary delay.

In the **middle stage of the visit, the student is generally expected to:

  • contribute actively to experiments, methodology development, analysis, or integration tasks;
  • demonstrate technical ownership and increasingly independent execution;
  • generate results, intermediate outputs, or building blocks toward publication or other deliverables;
  • communicate clearly about progress, bottlenecks, and next steps.

In the **final stage of the visit, the student is generally expected to:

  • consolidate the work carried out during the visit into reusable outputs;
  • help prepare manuscripts, technical reports, or structured handover material where relevant;
  • document the work clearly enough for continuity after departure;
  • ensure that the visit leaves a meaningful and traceable contribution rather than fragmented or inaccessible partial work.

These stages are indicative rather than rigid. The exact balance depends on the visit duration and project context. The important expectation is that the visit should show a credible trajectory from quick integration to meaningful contribution and orderly handover.

docs.title: Communication, Meetings, and Responsiveness
docs.text:
The visiting PhD student is expected to maintain regular communication with the supervisor and relevant team members.

Minimum expectations include:

  • attending scheduled one-to-one meetings, group meetings, project meetings, and other essential research discussions;
  • preparing adequately for meetings, including bringing updates, questions, and next steps;
  • responding to important emails and messages within a reasonable time frame;
  • informing the supervisor in advance when deadlines may not be met;
  • communicating absences, delays, obstacles, or personal constraints that materially affect project progress;
  • sharing information in a timely way rather than working in isolation on issues that affect the team.

As a general working norm, communication should be proactive. Repeated silence, lack of responsiveness, or lack of visibility over ongoing work is not acceptable in a collaborative research environment. In the case of a visiting appointment, this is especially important because delays in communication can consume a significant fraction of the visit and reduce the value of the collaboration.

docs.title: Working Style, Presence, and Time Commitment
docs.text:
A visiting PhD appointment is an outcomes-driven research engagement rather than a purely time-clocked role. At the same time, the visiting PhD student is expected to demonstrate a professional level of presence, engagement, and reliability.

The visiting PhD student is expected to:

  • maintain an appropriate and consistent level of engagement with the project and group;
  • be reasonably available during normal working periods for meetings and collaborative work;
  • spend sufficient time on the research to meet the expected progress and deliverables of the visit;
  • use flexible working arrangements responsibly, where permitted;
  • discuss in advance any recurring schedule constraints, remote-working plans, travel, or other factors that may affect availability.

A visiting appointment should not be treated as a passive affiliation or as a loosely defined presence in the group. The level of commitment should reflect the seriousness of the visit, the limited time available, and the expectation that the student is joining in order to contribute meaningfully rather than merely observe.

docs.title: Reporting and Documentation
docs.text:
Good research management requires proper documentation. The visiting PhD student is expected to maintain orderly records of relevant work, which may include:

  • experiment logs and methodological notes;
  • code repositories and version histories;

Good documentation is especially important in a visiting arrangement because the visit ends while the broader project usually continues. Documentation is therefore essential for continuity, reuse, authorship clarity, and later dissemination of the work.

docs.text:
The group values high-quality scholarly publication and responsible dissemination. The visiting PhD student is expected to contribute actively to manuscript preparation, revision, and submission when publication is an appropriate output of the work.

Because many visits are too short for a full publication cycle to be completed on site, the expectation is not necessarily that a paper will be fully published during the visit itself, but rather that the work carried out should be documented and structured in a way that supports a credible path toward publication where appropriate.

General expectations include:

  • authorship is earned through substantial contribution, not assumed by title or seniority alone;
