  • for publications arising primarily from the group's projects, funding, infrastructure, supervision, or intellectual direction, the supervisor must be listed as the last author, serve as the corresponding author, and be acknowledged as the principal investigator;
  • for other publications connected to work conducted within the group, authorship and author order should be discussed with the supervisor at an early stage and determined based on the nature and extent of contributions;
  • all listed authors are expected to contribute, review, and stand by the submitted work to a reasonable extent;
  • acknowledgement may be more appropriate than authorship for limited or peripheral contributions.

The visiting PhD student should not submit manuscripts, abstracts, or related outputs based on group work without prior discussion and approval from the supervisor and relevant co-authors. Because a visiting arrangement may involve coordination with the student's home supervisor and institution, authorship and publication planning should be discussed early to avoid misunderstandings later.

docs.title: Affiliation and acknowledgement
docs.text: All outputs arising from the visit are expected to include the correct institutional affiliation(s), required funding acknowledgements, and other sponsor- or project-specific statements where applicable. Where appropriate, outputs may need to reflect both the home institution and the host institution, depending on the nature of the contribution and the relevant policies.

docs.title: Data, Code, Intellectual Property, and Confidentiality
Data and code management
docs.text: Research data, code, models, annotations, figures, notes, and related materials produced as part of the visit must be handled responsibly and organized professionally. The visiting PhD student is expected to follow agreed project conventions for storage, naming, backup, version control, and documentation.

docs.title: Ownership and access
docs.text:
Materials generated within the scope of the visit, especially where tied to funded projects, collaborations, group infrastructure, or institutional resources, should be treated as project or institutional research assets rather than purely personal assets. The visiting PhD student must not withhold essential files, repositories, documentation, or access credentials needed for continuity of the research.

This point is especially important in a visiting appointment because the student will leave the group after a limited period. Departure should not create a situation in which relevant research materials remain inaccessible, undocumented, or dependent on personal accounts controlled externally.

docs.title: Confidentiality
docs.text:
The visiting PhD student may have access to unpublished results, confidential data, sponsor-sensitive information, reviewer discussions, internal proposals, software, or collaboration materials. Such information must not be disclosed, shared externally, or reused inappropriately without authorization.

This applies equally to communications with the home institution or external collaborators unless sharing has been agreed and is appropriate under the relevant project arrangements.

docs.title: Intellectual property and external use
docs.text:
Any potentially patentable, commercially sensitive, or otherwise protected outputs should be discussed before public disclosure. The visiting PhD student should not independently commercialize, license, distribute, or externally reuse project materials in ways that conflict with university policy, sponsor terms, collaboration agreements, or the legitimate interests of the group.

Where questions arise about the use of materials after the visit, those questions should be discussed explicitly rather than assumed.

docs.title: Collaboration and Team Conduct
docs.text:
The group values a constructive, respectful, and collaborative culture. The visiting PhD student is expected to contribute positively to that environment.

This includes:

  • interacting respectfully with students and colleagues;
  • giving and receiving technically sound and professional feedback;
  • avoiding dismissive, hostile, or inappropriate behavior;
  • recognizing that collaboration requires transparency and mutual accountability;
  • supporting a culture in which members can ask questions and seek guidance without fear of disrespect.

Because the visiting student joins an existing team for a limited period, it is particularly important to adapt constructively to the group's working culture, communicate respectfully, and contribute in a way that supports rather than disrupts the ongoing work of others.

docs.title: Mentoring and Support of Junior Researchers
docs.text:
Depending on project needs and the visiting student's expertise, the student may be asked to support the technical mentoring of master students, undergraduate students, visiting students, interns, or research assistants. This may include onboarding, providing technical guidance, reviewing code or manuscripts, helping design experiments, or advising on good research practice.

Such mentoring can be a valuable part of the visit where relevant and appropriate to the student's background. However, the primary purpose of the visit remains the focused advancement of the agreed research agenda. Any mentoring role should therefore be proportionate and should not unduly reduce the visiting student's ability to deliver the main objectives of the visit.

docs.title: Funding, Resources, Travel, and Expenses
docs.text:
Access to equipment, software, computing resources, travel, consumables, or project-related expenses is subject to budget availability, institutional rules, project scope, and supervisory approval.

The visiting PhD student is expected to:

  • use research funds and resources responsibly;
  • seek approval before incurring reimbursable expenses where required;
  • align conference and travel plans with project priorities, stage of the visit, and available resources;
  • understand that not all conferences, travel requests, or purchases can necessarily be supported;
  • handle institutional assets with care.

Where funding or travel support is limited, priorities should be determined based on the scientific value of the activity, the maturity of the work, and the overall needs of the project and group.

docs.title: Compliance, Ethics, and Safety
docs.text:
The visiting PhD student is expected to comply with all applicable requirements related to research integrity, human subjects, data governance, safety training, laboratory or workshop rules, fieldwork procedures, and any project-specific ethical or regulatory approvals.

No research activity requiring prior approval should begin before the relevant approvals are in place.

The fact that the student is visiting from another institution does not reduce the obligation to comply fully with host-institution requirements where applicable.

docs.title: Communication of Concerns and Resolution of Issues
docs.text:
A productive research environment requires open, respectful, and timely communication in both directions. The visiting PhD student is encouraged to raise concerns early whenever a problem, misunderstanding, or source of friction arises that may affect the research, working relationships, or the overall working environment.

Examples of concerns that should be communicated early include, but are not limited to:

  • misunderstandings regarding responsibilities, expectations, or priorities;
  • difficulties in communication or collaboration with other group members, students, staff, or external collaborators;
  • concerns about authorship, acknowledgement, contribution, or publication planning;
  • disagreement regarding research direction, technical decisions, or allocation of effort;
  • obstacles affecting progress, including lack of clarity, resource limitations, or personal circumstances that materially affect the work;
  • any situation in which an issue may worsen if left unaddressed.

In most cases, concerns should first be raised with the supervisor in a professional and constructive manner. The purpose of such discussion is to clarify expectations, understand the issue, and identify a reasonable path forward. Where appropriate, concerns involving other members of the group, the home institution, or collaborators may also be addressed through a joint discussion with the relevant parties.

Concerns raised in good faith will be treated seriously and respectfully. Open communication is considered an important part of maintaining a healthy, fair, and productive research environment.

docs.title: Performance Concerns and Escalation
docs.text:
If concerns arise regarding performance, conduct, communication, professionalism, fulfilment of responsibilities, or credible progress during the visit, the usual approach will be to discuss them directly and in good faith at an early stage. Depending on the issue, expectations may be clarified verbally or in writing, and reasonable time may be given for improvement where appropriate.

Examples of issues that may require attention include, but are not limited to:

  • persistent lack of progress without reasonable explanation;
  • repeated failure to meet agreed responsibilities or deadlines;
  • poor communication or prolonged unresponsiveness;
  • failure to document or share essential research materials appropriately;
  • serious lapses in professional conduct or research integrity;
  • behaviour that negatively affects the working environment or project continuity;
  • prolonged lack of meaningful contribution relative to the agreed objectives of the visit.

Nothing in this document limits the application of formal institutional procedures where required.

docs.title: End-of-Visit Responsibilities
docs.text:
Before the visit ends, the visiting PhD student is expected to support an orderly transition. This includes, as applicable:

  • organizing and transferring project files, repositories, data, models, and documentation;
  • summarizing the status of ongoing tasks, manuscripts, and deliverables;
  • returning equipment, access items, or other institutional property as required;
  • clarifying any remaining publication, authorship, or handover matters;
  • helping ensure that the work carried out during the visit is archived in a way that remains understandable and usable by the group.

Completion of the visit should not leave the project in a state where critical knowledge or materials are inaccessible. A successful visit should leave behind work that is technically usable, scientifically interpretable, and administratively clear enough to support continuity after departure.

docs.title: Mutual Understanding
docs.text: The goal of this document is not to create rigidity, but to set a professional foundation for an effective and productive research visit. The supervisor is committed to providing academic guidance, regular feedback, reasonable access to discussion, and support for the visiting student's scientific and professional growth during the stay. In return, the visiting PhD student is expected to engage seriously with the role, contribute actively and professionally within the limited duration of the visit, and uphold the standards outlined above.

docs.title: Postdoctoral Researcher Expectations and Working Principles
docs.text: This document outlines the main expectations, working norms, and professional responsibilities associated with a postdoctoral appointment in the **Digital Intelligence for Structures and Construction research group at NTU Singapore. It is intended to promote transparency, alignment, and a productive working relationship from the outset.

docs.title: Purpose of this Document
docs.text:
This document is provided together with the offer of a postdoctoral position to clarify expectations before the appointment begins. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, support mutual understanding, and help prevent avoidable misunderstandings at a later stage.

This document is not an employment contract and does not replace university, school, funding-agency, immigration, human resources, safety, ethics, or other institutional rules. Official NTU policies and the terms of the formal appointment remain controlling where applicable. Instead, this document sets out the research-group-level expectations that the postdoctoral researcher is expected to understand and accept before joining the group.

docs.title: Nature of the Appointment
docs.text:
A postdoctoral appointment in the group is a professional research position with a high degree of intellectual responsibility. The position is not limited to executing predefined tasks. It also requires initiative, sound judgement, accountability, and the ability to contribute meaningfully to the scientific direction, delivery, and visibility of the research.

The postdoctoral researcher is expected to:

  • contribute actively to the research objectives of the funded project(s) and the broader mission of the group;
  • work with professionalism, integrity, and collegiality;
  • make steady progress toward high-quality scholarly outputs;
  • help maintain an effective, respectful, and well-organized research environment;
  • operate at a level appropriate for an early-career independent researcher.

docs.title: General Professional Expectations
docs.text:
The postdoctoral researcher is expected to:

  • behave professionally and respectfully toward all students, staff, collaborators, and administrative personnel;
  • communicate in a timely, clear, and constructive manner;
  • act with honesty in research, reporting, authorship, data handling, and use of resources;
  • respect deadlines, commitments, and agreed responsibilities;
  • raise concerns early rather than allowing issues to accumulate;
  • represent the group and institution professionally in meetings, presentations, conferences, site visits, and online communication;
  • comply with relevant university and laboratory rules, including those related to safety, ethics, confidentiality, and responsible conduct of research.

docs.title: Ownership and initiative
docs.text:
The postdoctoral researcher is expected to take ownership of the assigned research agenda. This includes not only carrying out tasks, but also identifying technical bottlenecks, proposing solutions, planning next steps, and helping move the work forward without requiring excessive day-to-day direction.

The postdoctoral researcher should be able to:

  • understand the scientific goals, technical scope, and deliverables of the project;
  • plan and execute research activities with a reasonable degree of independence;
  • troubleshoot problems systematically and document solutions;
  • critically assess results rather than only generating them;
  • propose new ideas, extensions, and improvements where relevant.

docs.title: Quality of work
docs.text:
The group places strong emphasis on rigor and quality. The postdoctoral researcher is expected to produce work that is scientifically sound, technically careful, reproducible where relevant, and presented to a professional academic standard.

This includes expectations such as:

  • careful experimental design and documentation;
  • appropriate validation, benchmarking, and uncertainty awareness;
  • clean, understandable, and well-documented code and computational workflows;
  • organized data and version control practices;
  • accurate interpretation of results;
  • professional writing for manuscripts, reports, proposals, and presentations.

docs.title: Productivity and deliverables
docs.text:
The precise expected outputs depend on the project, contract duration, funding conditions, and stage of the work. However, the postdoctoral researcher is generally expected to contribute meaningfully to several of the following:

  • journal papers and conference papers;
  • technical reports, datasets, codebases, prototypes, or demonstrators;
  • internal project milestones and sponsor deliverables;
  • proposal development for future funding opportunities;
  • mentoring and technical support for students involved in related work;
  • presentations to collaborators, sponsors, or the research community.

Publication quantity alone is not the only performance measure, but a sustained lack of tangible research output, without justified reasons and prior discussion, will be considered a serious concern.

docs.title: Communication, Meetings, and Responsiveness
docs.text:
The postdoctoral researcher is expected to maintain regular communication with the supervisor and relevant team members.

Minimum expectations include:

  • attending scheduled one-to-one meetings, group meetings, project meetings, and other essential research discussions;
  • preparing adequately for meetings, including bringing updates, questions, and next steps;
  • responding to important emails and messages within a reasonable time frame;
  • informing the supervisor in advance when deadlines may not be met;
  • communicating absences, delays, obstacles, or personal constraints that materially affect project progress;
  • sharing information in a timely way rather than working in isolation on issues that affect the team.

As a general working norm, communication should be proactive. Repeated silence, lack of responsiveness, or lack of visibility over ongoing work is not acceptable in a collaborative research environment.

docs.title: Working Style, Presence, and Time Commitment
docs.text:
This is an outcomes-driven academic research position rather than a purely time-clocked role. At the same time, the postdoctoral researcher is expected to demonstrate a professional level of presence, engagement, and reliability.

The postdoctoral researcher is expected to:

  • maintain an appropriate and consistent level of engagement with the project and group;
  • be reasonably available during normal working periods for meetings and collaborative work;
  • spend sufficient time on the research to meet expected progress and deliverables;
  • use flexible working arrangements responsibly, where permitted;
  • discuss in advance any recurring schedule constraints, remote-working plans, travel, or other factors that may affect availability.

A postdoctoral appointment should not be treated as a passive holding position or as a role with minimal engagement. The level of commitment should reflect the professional nature of the appointment and the expectations associated with funded academic research.

docs.title: Reporting and Documentation
docs.text:
Good research management requires proper documentation. The postdoctoral researcher is expected to maintain orderly records of relevant work, which may include:

  • experiment logs and methodological notes;
  • code repositories and version histories;
  • structured storage of datasets, models, scripts, figures, and reports;
  • summaries of results, decisions, and action items;
  • progress updates suitable for internal tracking or sponsor reporting.

If requested, the postdoctoral researcher should provide concise periodic written updates on progress, obstacles, priorities, and next steps. Research materials generated within the appointment should be stored in a manner accessible to the group and not only on personal devices or private accounts.

docs.title: Publication culture
docs.text: The group values high-quality scholarly publication and responsible dissemination. The postdoctoral researcher is expected to contribute actively to manuscript preparation, revision, and submission when publication is an appropriate output of the work.

docs.title: Authorship principles
docs.text:
Authorship should reflect substantial intellectual or technical contribution and should follow accepted academic norms as well as any relevant institutional or journal guidance. Authorship order will depend on the nature and extent of contributions and will be discussed in good faith.

General expectations include:

  • authorship is earned through substantial contribution, not assumed by title or seniority alone;
  • first authorship typically requires a leading contribution to the core research and manuscript preparation;
  • for publications arising primarily from the group's projects, funding, infrastructure, supervision, or intellectual direction, the supervisor must be listed as the last author, serve as the corresponding author, and be acknowledged as the principal investigator;
  • for other publications connected to work conducted within the group, authorship and author order should be discussed with the supervisor at an early stage and determined based on the nature and extent of contributions;
  • all listed authors are expected to contribute, review, and stand by the submitted work to a reasonable extent;
  • acknowledgement may be more appropriate than authorship for limited or peripheral contributions.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text: DISC Lab advances the digitalization, monitoring, and automation of infrastructure and construction through the integration of computer vision, machine learning, robotics, and extended reality.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text:
The lab aims to develop scientifically rigorous and practically relevant methods for:

  • objective condition assessment of structures
  • semantic understanding of built environments
  • digital representations of assets across scales
  • AI-assisted inspection and decision support
  • perception-enabled construction automation

docs.title: Core values
docs.text:
• Scientific rigor: methods must be technically defensible and well validated.
  • Reproducibility: all major outputs should be traceable and repeatable.
  • Practical relevance: research should connect to real civil engineering problems.
  • Interdisciplinarity: combine structural engineering with AI, vision, XR, and robotics.
  • Professional conduct: documentation, communication, and collaboration must be reliable.

docs.title: DISCLab Templates
docs.text:
This section collects the main templates used across the DISC Laboratory for documents, reports, presentations, and other research-related materials.

The purpose of these templates is to promote consistency, save time, and ensure that commonly used documents follow a clear and professional structure aligned with the lab’s practices.

docs.title: What you will find here
docs.text:
The templates provided in this section may include:

  • LaTeX templates for internal and formal documents;
  • report and presentation templates;
  • reusable formats for research documentation and communication.

docs.title: How to use these templates
docs.text:
Each template page contains a short description of the document, together with links to download the source files and, when available, a PDF version for quick reference.

In general:

  • use these templates as starting points;
  • create a copy before editing;
  • avoid modifying the master version directly unless you are updating the official template for the lab.

docs.title: General intention
docs.text: The templates are intended to support efficient and consistent work across the lab. They are not meant to restrict flexibility unnecessarily, but to provide a reliable base that members can adapt to specific needs while maintaining a coherent lab identity.

docs.text:
The CV template is designed to present academic and professional information in a clear, structured, and professional format. It is suitable for:

The template follows a clean, single-column layout with a conservative and readable style aligned with academic standards.

docs.title: Writing and Publications
docs.text:
This section defines the standards, practices, and expectations related to scientific writing and publication within the DISC Laboratory.

The goal is to ensure that research outputs from the group are:

  • scientifically rigorous;
  • clearly communicated;
  • well-structured and reproducible;
  • aligned with high academic standards.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This section covers:

  • manuscript preparation and structuring;
  • authorship and contribution principles;
  • internal review workflows;
  • publication strategies (conference vs journal);
  • formatting and presentation of scientific content.

docs.title: General philosophy
docs.text:
Writing is considered an integral part of research, not a final step. A well-written paper reflects clear thinking, strong methodology, and careful interpretation of results.

Members are expected to:

  • treat writing as part of the research process;
  • aim for clarity, precision, and technical correctness;
  • maintain consistency between experiments, results, and claims;
  • follow structured workflows for drafting and reviewing manuscripts.

docs.title: Structure of this section
docs.text:
The documents in this section provide guidance on:

  • how to organize and prepare manuscripts;
  • how contributions translate into authorship;
  • how internal review and submission should be handled;
  • how to choose appropriate publication venues;
  • how to format papers and supporting materials.

These guidelines are intended to support high-quality output while maintaining consistency across the lab.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
Authorship should reflect substantial intellectual or technical contribution to the research work.

Authorship is not granted based on position, seniority, or administrative role alone.

docs.title: Contribution areas
docs.text:
Typical contributions that may justify authorship include:

  • conceptualization of the research problem;
  • methodology design;
  • data curation and dataset preparation;
  • software development and implementation;
  • validation and experimentation;
  • formal analysis and interpretation of results;
  • writing (drafting or significant revision);
  • supervision and technical guidance;
  • funding acquisition (when coupled with scientific contribution).

docs.title: Minimum expectation for co-authorship
docs.text:
A co-author is expected to:

  • contribute meaningfully to the scientific content;
  • review and approve the manuscript before submission;
  • be able to explain and defend the contribution at a reasonable level.

docs.title: Authorship order
docs.text:
Authorship order should reflect the level and nature of contribution:

  • first author: main contributor to the research and manuscript;
  • middle authors: supporting contributors;
  • last author: typically the principal investigator (PI), responsible for supervision and research direction.

docs.title: Lab-specific expectation
docs.text:
• For work conducted within DISC Lab projects, the PI is expected to be the last author and corresponding author;
  • Authorship discussions should happen early, especially for multi-student or collaborative projects;
  • Changes in authorship must be discussed transparently with all contributors.

docs.title: Good practice
docs.text:
• clarify contributions early and revisit when needed;
  • avoid adding authors with minimal contribution;
  • acknowledge contributors appropriately when authorship is not justified.

Authorship should reflect fairness, transparency, and scientific integrity.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text: The choice between conference and journal publication should be driven by scientific fit, maturity of the work, and communication goals, not only by prestige.

docs.title: Use conferences when
docs.text:
• the work is timely and benefits from rapid dissemination;
  • early feedback from the community is strategically valuable;
  • the venue is relevant to the target research community;
  • the work represents a well-defined but possibly intermediate contribution;
  • visibility and discussion are priorities.

docs.title: Use journals when
docs.text:
• the work is mature, validated, and complete;
  • the contribution requires deeper methodological or theoretical development;
  • extensive experiments and analysis are needed;
  • archival impact and long-term reference value are important.

docs.title: Combined strategy
docs.text:
In many cases, a combined approach is appropriate:

  • conference → early version, feedback, visibility;
  • journal → extended version with deeper validation and contributions.

docs.title: Lab recommendation
docs.text:
• prioritize scientific alignment with the venue over impact factor alone;
  • ensure that the level of rigor matches the target venue;
  • avoid fragmenting contributions into multiple weak papers;
  • aim for coherent, meaningful contributions rather than maximizing publication count.

docs.title: Key consideration
docs.text: The objective is not only to publish, but to produce clear, credible, and impactful research contributions.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text:
Figures are central to communicating research. They should be clear, informative, and self-contained.

A reader should understand the key message of a figure without needing extensive explanation.

docs.title: Key expectations
docs.text:
• figures must be readable at publication size;
  • visualizations must support the main claims of the paper;
  • style must be consistent across all figures.

docs.title: Readability
docs.text:
• use sufficiently large fonts;
  • ensure good contrast between elements;
  • avoid overcrowded visuals.

docs.title: Consistency
docs.text:
• use consistent color schemes across figures;
  • maintain consistent labeling conventions;
  • use the same scale when comparing results.

docs.title: Formats
docs.text:
• prefer vector formats (.pdf, .svg) when possible;
  • use high-resolution images for raster formats.

docs.title: Specific guidelines for DISC Lab work
Computer vision outputs
docs.text:
• clearly distinguish predictions vs ground truth;
  • use consistent color coding for classes;
  • overlay results in a way that preserves interpretability.

docs.title: 3D visualizations
docs.text:
• use clean and professional color palettes;
  • avoid excessive shading or distracting effects;
  • highlight key elements (e.g., damage regions, structures).

docs.title: Plots and graphs
docs.text:
• label axes clearly (including units);
  • include legends when necessary;
  • avoid unnecessary gridlines or decorations.

docs.title: Figure captions
docs.text:
• captions should explain what the figure shows;
  • include context and key takeaway;
  • avoid overly long descriptions.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• low-resolution images;
  • inconsistent color schemes;
  • unclear labels;
  • figures that do not support a clear message.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: Figures are often the most visible part of a paper. Invest time in making them clear and professional.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
The internal review process ensures that all manuscripts meet the lab’s standards in terms of:

  • clarity and structure;
  • scientific rigor;
  • consistency between methods, results, and claims;
  • readiness for submission.

docs.title: Suggested workflow
docs.text:
1. Lead author prepares a complete draft
  2. includes full structure, figures, and main message;

  3. results and conclusions must be consistent.

  4. Co-authors review

  5. focus on technical correctness and positioning;

  6. provide structured and constructive feedback.

  7. PI first review

  8. evaluates scientific contribution, clarity, and overall quality;

  9. identifies major issues or required changes.

  10. Revision by lead author

  11. consolidate feedback;

  12. ensure consistency across sections.

  13. PI final review

  14. confirms readiness for submission;

  15. validates positioning and contribution.

  16. Submission preparation

  17. prepare cover letter and required documents;

  18. ensure formatting compliance with venue guidelines.

  19. Final check

  20. verify figures, references, and formatting;

  21. confirm all authors approve submission.

  22. Submission

  23. lead author submits with PI approval and credentials.

docs.title: Minimum rule
docs.text:
Do not circulate incomplete drafts for broad review if:

  • the structure is unclear;
  • figures are missing or not interpretable;
  • the main message is not defined.

docs.title: Expected behavior
docs.text:
• reviews should be timely and constructive;
  • feedback should be specific and actionable;
  • the lead author is responsible for consolidating and implementing revisions.

The goal is to improve the paper efficiently while maintaining high quality standards.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This checklist ensures that the manuscript is ready for submission and meets the lab’s quality standards.

docs.title: Content checklist
docs.text:
• the problem and contribution are clearly stated;
  • the methodology is well described;
  • results support the claims;
  • limitations are acknowledged where relevant.

docs.title: Figures and tables
docs.text:
• all figures are high quality and readable;
  • captions are clear and informative;
  • figures are referenced and explained in the text;
  • tables are consistent and properly formatted.

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
• dataset is clearly described;
  • experimental setup is documented;
  • results are traceable to experiments;
  • configuration details are available.

docs.title: Writing quality
docs.text:
• grammar and language are correct;
  • terminology is consistent;
  • no redundant or unclear sections;
  • logical flow between sections.

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
• template requirements are followed;
  • references are correctly formatted;
  • figures and tables are properly placed;
  • submission guidelines of the venue are respected.

docs.title: Authorship and approvals
docs.text:
• all authors have reviewed the manuscript;
  • authorship order is agreed;
  • PI has approved submission.

docs.title: Final checks
docs.text:
• no missing figures or references;
  • no placeholder text remains;
  • PDF compiles correctly.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: Submission should only occur when the paper is complete, coherent, and validated.

docs.title: General recommendations
docs.text:
• LaTeX templates are preferred;
  • use conference/journal templates when provided;
  • otherwise, use the lab paper template as a baseline.

docs.title: Figures and tables
docs.text:
• figures must be high quality and clearly readable;
  • prefer vectorized figures tools (e.g., inkscape) and formats (e.g., .pdf) when possible;
  • ensure consistency in style, fonts, and colors;
  • use clear naming conventions:
  • figures: fig_short_description.pdf
  • tables: tab_short_description
  • appropriate and ethical use of GenAI for assistance on framework figures is encoraged (e.g., feedback of composition; generation of individual components)

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• use consistent citation styles;
  • ensure all references are complete and correctly formatted;
  • guarantee to have display doi and web link;
  • use descriptive naming for .bib entries (e.g., YearAuthor_keyword).

docs.title: Response to reviewers
docs.text:
• address comments one by one;
  • copy each reviewer comment and respond explicitly;
  • use deferent format for reviewers comments and your replies (e.g., italic, blue);
  • clearly indicate changes made in the manuscript;
  • maintain a professional, respectful and constructive tone;
  • it is ecoraged to accept and incorporates as much as possible the reviewers' comments;
  • only rebute a comment if it is completely out of context.

docs.title: Structure and clarity
docs.text:
• ensure logical flow between sections;
  • avoir being overwordy --- simple and straight forward is prefered;
  • avoid redundancy;
  • make figures and captions self-contained;
  • ensure terminology is consistent throughout the paper;
  • ensure to define all the acronyms.

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text:
Templates should reduce formatting friction, not replace scientific thinking.

Good formatting supports clarity, but the primary focus should always remain on the quality and coherence of the scientific contribution.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text: Publications should follow a coherent research trajectory, not a collection of unrelated outputs.

docs.title: Early stage
docs.text:
• literature review;
  • baseline implementations;
  • initial conference paper (optional).

docs.title: Mid stage
docs.text:
• methodological contributions;
  • conference papers;
  • initial journal submission.

docs.title: Final stage
docs.text:
• mature contributions;
  • journal papers;
  • thesis consolidation.

docs.title: Strategy recommendations
docs.text:
• aim for quality over quantity;
  • avoid splitting contributions unnecessarily;
  • align publications with thesis structure.

docs.title: Venue selection
docs.text:
• choose venues based on relevance to the research;
  • consider audience and impact;
  • balance speed vs rigor.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: A good publication strategy supports both scientific contribution and career development.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text:
Research outputs must be traceable, reproducible, and well-documented.

A reader (or lab member) should be able to understand how results were obtained and, where possible, reproduce them.

docs.title: Key expectations
docs.text:
• experiments must be documented;
  • datasets must be clearly described;
  • results must be linked to specific configurations and methods.

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
• dataset source and description;
  • preprocessing steps;
  • dataset splits (train/validation/test).

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• model architecture or methodology;
  • key parameters and configurations;
  • training or processing procedures.

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
• experiment setup;
  • variations between experiments;
  • evaluation metrics used.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• quantitative results (with clear metrics);
  • qualitative results when relevant;
  • comparison with baselines.

docs.title: Traceability
docs.text:
Each result should be traceable to:

  • a dataset version;
  • a code version;
  • a configuration file;
  • an experiment record.

docs.title: Link with lab structure
docs.text:
• experiments → 04_Experiments/
  • models → 03_Models/
  • results → 05_Results/

All reported results should correspond to stored and documented artifacts.

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
• missing details about data or preprocessing;
  • unclear experimental setup;
  • results that cannot be linked to a specific experiment;
  • inconsistent reporting across sections.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: Reproducibility is not optional. It is a core aspect of scientific quality.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text: A strong paper tells a clear and coherent story about a problem and its solution.

docs.title: Key elements
docs.text:
A good research narrative includes:

  1. motivation
  2. problem definition
  3. gap in existing work
  4. proposed solution
  5. validation
  6. implications

docs.title: Positioning the contribution
docs.text:
• clearly state what is new;
  • explain why it matters;
  • relate it to existing work.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
• each section should logically follow the previous one;
  • avoid disconnected ideas;
  • maintain a clear thread throughout the paper.

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
• unclear contribution;
  • weak connection between sections;
  • excessive technical detail without context;
  • lack of focus.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: A paper is not just a collection of results. It is a structured argument.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reviewing papers is an essential part of academic development. It helps improve critical thinking and understanding of research quality.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text:
A good review is:

  • objective;
  • constructive;
  • focused on scientific content.

docs.title: Contribution
docs.text:
• is the problem clearly defined?
  • is the contribution meaningful and original?

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• is the approach sound?
  • are assumptions reasonable?

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
• are experiments well designed?
  • are results convincing and properly validated?

docs.title: Clarity
docs.text:
• is the paper well structured?
  • is the writing clear and precise?

docs.title: Providing feedback
docs.text:
• be specific and actionable;
  • distinguish major vs minor comments;
  • suggest improvements when possible.

docs.title: Tone
docs.text:
• remain professional and respectful;
  • avoid dismissive or vague criticism;
  • focus on improving the work.

docs.title: Internal reviews in the lab
docs.text:
When reviewing papers within the lab:

  • prioritize technical correctness;
  • ensure consistency between sections;
  • check alignment between results and claims.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• focusing only on minor issues;
  • not identifying core weaknesses;
  • providing vague feedback;
  • not reading the paper carefully.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: Reviewing is part of being a researcher. Treat it as a serious and constructive activity.

docs.title: General principle
docs.text:
Scientific writing should prioritize clarity, precision, and structure over complexity.

A well-written paper reflects clear thinking and a strong understanding of the problem.

docs.title: Key expectations
docs.text:
• write clearly and directly;
  • avoid unnecessary complexity;
  • ensure every sentence contributes to the message;
  • maintain consistency in terminology.

docs.title: Structure of a strong paper
docs.text:
A typical paper should clearly communicate:

  1. Context and motivation
  2. Problem or gap
  3. Proposed approach
  4. Validation and results
  5. Key contributions and implications

docs.title: Clarity
docs.text:
• use simple and precise language;
  • avoid overly long sentences;
  • define terms when first introduced.

docs.title: Consistency
docs.text:
• use consistent terminology throughout the paper;
  • avoid switching between synonyms for the same concept;
  • ensure figures, text, and tables use the same naming conventions.

docs.title: Conciseness
docs.text:
• avoid redundant explanations;
  • remove unnecessary words or repetitions;
  • focus on what is essential.

docs.title: Technical precision
docs.text:
• ensure claims are supported by results;
  • avoid vague statements (e.g., “significant improvement” without quantification);
  • report relevant metrics and conditions.

docs.title: Common mistakes to avoid
docs.text:
• unclear problem statement;
  • missing connection between results and conclusions;
  • inconsistent terminology;
  • overly descriptive but not analytical writing;
  • figures that are not explained in the text.

docs.title: Final note
docs.text: Good writing is iterative. Expect to revise multiple times to achieve clarity and coherence.

docs.title: Project Templates
docs.text: This section contains starter templates that members can copy into their projects.

docs.title: Experiment Log Template
docs.text:
# Experiment ID
EXP-YYYYMMDD-name-v01

## Objective

## Owner

## Date

## Code commit

## Dataset version

## Configuration

docs.title: README Template
docs.text:
# Project title

## Summary
Short description of the project.

## Objective
What problem is being solved?

## Repository structure
Explain main folders.

## Setup
How to install dependencies.

## Usage
How to run the main workflow.

## Data
Where the data comes from and how it is organized.

## Results
Where outputs are saved.

## Maintainers
Names and roles.

docs.title: Minimum contents
docs.text:
• README.md
  • src/
  • configs/
  • data/
  • experiments/
  • results/
  • docs/
  • environment file

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Create one canonical lab template repository and update it when practices improve.

docs.title: Research Guidelines
docs.text: This section standardizes how projects are structured and executed across the lab.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• reduce onboarding time
  • improve reproducibility
  • make collaboration easier
  • ensure minimum documentation quality

docs.title: General principles
docs.text:
• Code must be readable before it is clever.
  • Prefer modular functions over long scripts.
  • Every reusable project should include a clear entry point.

docs.title: Python standards
docs.text:
• follow PEP 8 where reasonable
  • use descriptive variable and function names
  • include docstrings for non-trivial functions
  • avoid hard-coded file paths
  • store configurable values in config files

docs.title: Recommended tooling
docs.text:
• formatter: black
  • linter: ruff
  • type hints when useful
  • environment management with venv, conda, or poetry

docs.title: ML-specific guidance
docs.text:
• separate training, evaluation, and inference code
  • record random seeds
  • save configuration used for each run
  • never overwrite model checkpoints silently

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
• datasets must be traceable to their source
  • annotation versions must be documented
  • derived datasets must be reproducible from raw data whenever possible

docs.title: Minimum dataset documentation
docs.text:
• acquisition source
  • ownership and usage restrictions
  • date of acquisition
  • preprocessing steps
  • annotation protocol
  • train/validation/test split rationale

docs.title: Recommended files
docs.text:
data/
├── raw/
├── processed/
├── annotations/
└── dataset_card.md

docs.title: Dataset card should include
docs.text:
• task definition
  • class definitions
  • known limitations
  • imbalance issues
  • labeling quality notes

docs.title: Experiment Tracking
docs.text: Every experiment must be recoverable.

docs.title: Minimum information to record
docs.text:
• project name
  • experiment ID
  • date and owner
  • code commit hash
  • dataset version
  • configuration file
  • hardware used
  • training duration
  • output checkpoint path
  • main metrics
  • short conclusion

docs.title: Recommended experiment ID format
docs.text: EXP-YYYYMMDD-short-description-v01

docs.title: Acceptable tools
docs.text:
• Markdown logs in the repository
  • CSV experiment table
  • Weights & Biases or MLflow
  • structured YAML or JSON summaries

docs.title: Files and folders
docs.text:
Use lowercase names with hyphens or underscores. Avoid spaces.

Examples: - bridge-crack-segmentation - dataset_card.md - exp_20260330_yolo11s_1024.yaml

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
Include model, data version, and key setting.

Example: 

exp_20260330_yolo11s_dacl10k_v2_img1024

docs.title: Figures and tables
docs.text:
Use names that reveal content.

Examples: - fig_bridge_crack_qualitative_examples.png - tab_ablation_resolution_vs_map.csv

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text: Each research project should have a predictable structure.

docs.title: Recommended top-level layout
docs.text:
project-name/
├── README.md
├── docs/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── interim/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── external/
├── src/
├── configs/
├── experiments/
├── models/
├── results/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
└── references/

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• raw/ data must remain immutable.
  • src/ contains reusable code, not one-off scripts.
  • configs/ stores all experiment settings.
  • experiments/ contains logs and run-level metadata.
  • results/ stores generated outputs and figures.
  • reports/ stores project memos, internal summaries, and deliverables.

docs.title: Minimum required files
docs.text:
• README.md
  • one environment file (requirements.txt, environment.yml, or similar)
  • one configuration example
  • one usage example

docs.title: Student Onboarding
docs.text: This section should reduce uncertainty for new lab members and accelerate their first weeks in the group.

docs.title: Typical access needs
docs.text:
• institutional email and drives
  • GitHub organization or repositories
  • GPU servers
  • shared lab storage
  • issue tracker or project board
  • software licenses when applicable

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Access should be granted only after the member has read the relevant usage and data handling pages.

docs.title: Core expectations
docs.text:
• communicate early when blocked
  • document your work continuously
  • maintain your repositories and files cleanly
  • be reliable with deadlines and meetings
  • ask questions after making a serious first attempt

docs.title: Professional expectation
docs.text: Good research practice includes good documentation, not only good technical output.

docs.title: First Week Checklist
docs.text:
• Meet with PI or supervisor
  • Obtain access to storage, repositories, and shared documents
  • Install required software
  • Read the lab wiki core pages
  • Review project README and current tasks
  • Confirm communication channels
  • Confirm reporting schedule
  • Clarify short-term deliverables for the first month

docs.title: Typical tools
docs.text:
• Python
  • Git
  • VS Code
  • CUDA toolkit when needed
  • Docker when needed
  • Unity for XR projects
  • COLMAP / Meshroom / Open3D for 3D workflows

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: Maintain one installation page per platform if the lab supports both Windows and Linux workflows.

docs.title: XR Development
docs.text: This section defines recommended practices for XR applications developed in the lab, especially for Unity and HoloLens-based workflows.

docs.title: Deployment checklist
docs.text:
• correct Unity version recorded
  • target SDK documented
  • package dependencies recorded
  • build settings exported
  • test device and OS version recorded
  • deployment mode documented

docs.title: Recommended practice
docs.text: Maintain one page per app with exact build steps and known issues.

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
• export the exact model variant used in deployment
  • validate inference consistency between training and deployment environments
  • record pre-processing and post-processing steps
  • document input tensor shape and normalization

docs.title: Minimum validation
docs.text:
• one offline test on desktop
  • one deployment test on target XR device
  • one qualitative comparison against expected output

docs.title: Key constraints to monitor
docs.text:
• inference latency
  • memory use
  • frame rate stability
  • thermal behavior
  • resolution trade-offs

docs.title: Reporting recommendation
docs.text: All XR papers and reports should state the hardware platform, approximate latency, and any major trade-offs made for real-time or near real-time performance.

docs.title: Recommended structure
docs.text:
• Assets/Scripts/
  • Assets/Scenes/
  • Assets/Prefabs/
  • Assets/Materials/
  • Assets/Models/
  • Assets/StreamingAssets/
  • Assets/Plugins/
  • Assets/Resources/ only when justified

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• keep scenes minimal and modular
  • separate UI, inference, and visualization logic
  • document all external plugins and versions
